control_description: The entity provides notice to data subjects about its privacy practices to meet the entity's objectives related to privacy.

evidence_files.description: Helix Software external privacy policy stating data sharing practices
GDPR Article 30 Record of Processing Activities (ROPA) listing all data processing activities and third-party processors

prompt: You are conducting a SOC 2 readiness assessment for Helix Software. Review the privacy policy and the data processing activity records (ROPA). Cross-reference the two documents to assess whether the privacy notice accurately reflects the organization's actual data processing and sharing practices. Identify any contradictions, omissions, or inconsistencies between what the policy tells data subjects and what actually occurs.

findings.title: Privacy policy claims no third-party data sharing but ROPA lists three third-party processors
findings.description: Section 4 of the privacy policy states: 'Helix Software does not sell, rent, or share your personal information with third parties for their own purposes. Your data remains exclusively within the Helix Software environment.' However, the data processing activity records (ROPA) list three active third-party data processors: (1) Amplitude Inc. for product analytics (PROC-008), receiving user behavioral data, IP addresses, and device identifiers; (2) SendGrid (Twilio) for transactional email delivery (PROC-009), receiving email addresses, names, and communication content; and (3) Salesforce for CRM and customer relationship management (PROC-010), receiving customer names, email addresses, company information, support history, and usage metrics. This is a direct contradiction between the privacy notice and actual data processing practices. Data subjects are being told their data is not shared when it is actively transmitted to three external processors.
findings.expected_reasoning: The privacy policy Section 4 explicitly states no third-party sharing. The ROPA shows three processors (Amplitude, SendGrid, Salesforce) actively receiving personal data. This is not a nuance issue — the policy categorically denies sharing while the ROPA documents it. The privacy notice is materially inaccurate.

findings.title: Cross-border data transfer to Amplitude US servers not disclosed in privacy policy
findings.description: The ROPA entry PROC-008 (Amplitude analytics) shows data is transferred to Amplitude's US-based servers (transfer_destination: 'United States'). The privacy policy Section 6 ('Data Location') states: 'All personal data is processed and stored within the European Economic Area.' This is another direct contradiction — personal data is being transferred outside the EEA to the United States via Amplitude without disclosure to data subjects and without documented transfer safeguards (SCCs, adequacy decision) in the policy.
findings.expected_reasoning: The ROPA shows Amplitude receives data in the US (PROC-008, transfer_destination='United States'). The privacy policy claims all data stays within the EEA. This undisclosed international transfer is both a privacy notice failure and a potential GDPR Chapter V violation.

findings.title: Salesforce CRM processing includes usage metrics not covered by stated data collection categories
findings.description: The ROPA entry PROC-010 shows Salesforce receives 'usage_metrics' and 'support_history' alongside standard customer contact information. The privacy policy Section 2 ('Information We Collect') does not list 'usage metrics' or 'product usage data' as a category of personal information collected or shared. Data subjects are not informed that their product usage patterns are being shared with a third-party CRM system and potentially used for sales and account management purposes.
findings.expected_reasoning: PROC-010 shows usage_metrics flowing to Salesforce. The privacy policy's data collection section does not disclose that product usage metrics are collected and shared with CRM platforms. This is an undisclosed data processing activity.
